coredata.dc:title: Molecular characterizations of nanodiamond and related carbon-based nanomaterials in the human cell line and zebrafish models
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Min
Chang, Chien
Yeh, Tseng-Yu
Lee, Tung-Yu
Lee, Kuen-Chan
Cho, Er-Chieh
coredata.dc:description: Carbon-based nanomaterials (CBNs) have been considered as prospective and frontline materials for not only industrial but also biomedical applications. With unique chemical and physical properties including mechanical, electrical, and thermal diversity, CBNs recently attracted great attention for drug delivery system development. However, the biocompatibility of CBNs structural diversity in human beings is still obscure. Moreover, the general molecular and genetic regulations of CBNs within human cells have not been examined and compared before. In this study, it would be the first time that six different carboxylated CBNs were introduced, including nanodiamond (ND), graphene (Gra), single-wall carbon nanotube (SW), graphene quantum dot (GQD), and two fullerenes (C60 and C70), covering different kinds of nanostructure, from 0-dimension to 3-dimension, were compared and investigated in different systems. We characterized the CBNs, examined their cellular biocompatibility in human kidney (293T) and lung (IMR90) cells, and investigated the cell cycle regulations and cell death pathways activation mediated by CBNs. The transcriptome profile of human cells exposed to CBNs was examined, and among all, ND exposed samples exhibited diverse regulatory patterns, and therefore the related complex network-and-pathway regulations of the CBN in human cells were further analyzed. In the zebrafish embryo model, all CBNs did not influence either the survival or hatching rates of the embryos, demonstrating the great biocompatibility of CBNs. This study evidences that carboxylated CBNs with various dimensional structures exhibited different molecular modulations toward human cells, and provides novel insights into the safety evaluations of CBNs for future applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanodiamond
Carbon-based nanomaterials
Biocompatibility
Cell cycle analysis
Cell death pathways
RNA-sequencing analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Previous studies of impacts and potential toxicity of CBNs
Gaps in knowledge and the rationale of this study
Cell cycle analysis by flow cytometer
RNA extraction, RNA quality evaluation, and RNA sequencing (RNA-seq) analysis
Biocompatibility evaluation of CBNs in human cells
Cellular and molecular regulations of CBNs in cells
RNA-sequencing analysis of human cells treated with CBNs
Genetic regulations of human cells mediated by ND treatment
Biocompatibility examination of CBNs in the zebrafish model

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Science and Technology Council (NSTC 113-2221-E-152-001, NSTC 112-2628-B-038-009-MY3), Taiwan. This work was also supported by the \u201CTMU Research Center of Cancer Translational Medicine\u201D from The Featured Areas Research Center Program within the framework of the Higher Education Sprout Project by the Ministry of Education (MOE) in Taiwan. We thank the Core Facility Centers at Taipei Medical University and National Taipei University of Education for the technical supports. We would also like to thank the Core Laboratory of Zebrafish, Office of R&D at Taipei Medical University for providing the wild type fish.

This research was supported by the National Science and Technology Council ( NSTC 111-2314-B-152-001 , NSTC 112-2221-E-152-001 , NSTC 112-2628-B-038-009-MY3 ), Taiwan. This work was also supported by the \u201CTMU Research Center of Cancer Translational Medicine\u201D from The Featured Areas Research Center Program within the framework of the Higher Education Sprout Project by the Ministry of Education (MOE) in Taiwan. We thank the Core Facility Center at Taipei Medical University for the technical supports.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CBNs by TEM.
Morphological identification of carboxylated CBNs with 0D ~ 3D dimensional structure through transmission electron microscope (TEM).
Biocompatibility evaluation of CBNs in human cells.

(A) MTT assay of 293 T cells after co-incubated with CBNs at indicated concentrations for 24 h. (B) Cell morphological images of 293 T cells after co-incubated with CBNs for 24 h. 40× microscopy magnification was applied. (C) MTT assay of IMR90 cells after co-incubated with CBNs at indicated concentrations for 24 h. (D) Cell morphological images of IMR90 cells after co-incubated with CBNs for 24 h. The scale bar stands for 100 μm.
Cell cycle regulations of CBNs in cells.
(A) Histogram and (B) bar chart analysis of the cell cycle profile in 293 T cells after co-incubated with 20 μg/mL of CBNs for 24 h.
Cellular mechanisms investigation of CBNs in cells.

293 T cells were treated with 20 μg/mL of CBNs for 24 h, and then harvested for protein expression analysis. (A) Western blot analysis of death pathway-related proteins. (B) Western blot analysis of cell cycle regulatory factors. GAPDH was shown as loading control for western blot assays.
Principal component analysis (PCA).

293 T cells were treated with 20 μg/mL of carboxylated CBNs for 24 h and then harvested. RNA was extracted for RNA-sequencing analysis. PCA plot analysis evaluates and visualizes the expression difference among various sample conditions.
Heatmap analysis.
Heatmap analysis displays a subset of genes that exhibited the most variance was selected for clustering analysis. The number of genes clustered was 250.
Volcano plot analysis.

Gene groups that differ significantly between the pairs of experimental conditions and control are revealed in the volcano plot. The volcano plot shows the distribution of all genes according to fold-change (log2 of fold-change on x-axis) and significance (negative logarithm of the P-value on the y-axis). The red dotted line is the P-value cut-off (0.05). The green line is the log2-fold-change cut-off (1,−1), which is related to the fold change of cut-off (2, 0.5). Differentially expressed genes are presented as blue dotted, filter by log2-fold-change (1, −1) and P-value (0.05).
Genetic regulations of human cells mediated by ND.

Names of the 58 cell cycle molecules were analyzed by STRING protein-protein interaction prediction software (version: 12.0), setting as no >50 interactors, and then the network is clustered by Markov Cluster Algorithm (MCL clustering) through STRING software.
In vivo biocompatibility evaluation in the zebrafish model.

(A) Survival rate analysis of zebrafish embryos and larvae after exposed with CBNs at different time points. (B) Hatching rate analysis of zebrafish embryo co-incubated with CBNs. (C) Body length of zebrafish larvae exposed under CBNs at concentration of 10 and 20 μg/mL at 96 h post fertilization (hpf). (D) Morphological images of zebrafish larvae under CBNs concentration of 10 and 20 μg/mL at 96 hpf.
Number of differentially expressed genes (DEGs).
Examples of enrichment GO biological process (ND/ctrl).

row.entry: GO:0043524 ~ negative regulation of neuron apoptotic process

head.ce:title: Molecular characterizations of nanodiamond and related carbon-based nanomaterials in the human cell line and zebrafish models

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: E.-C. Cho, School of Pharmacy, College of Pharmacy, Taipei Medical University, 250 Wuxing Street, Taipei City 110, Taiwan.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon-based nanomaterials (CBNs) have been considered as prospective and frontline materials for not only industrial but also biomedical applications. With unique chemical and physical properties including mechanical, electrical, and thermal diversity, CBNs recently attracted great attention for drug delivery system development. However, the biocompatibility of CBNs structural diversity in human beings is still obscure. Moreover, the general molecular and genetic regulations of CBNs within human cells have not been examined and compared before. In this study, it would be the first time that six different carboxylated CBNs were introduced, including nanodiamond (ND), graphene (Gra), single-wall carbon nanotube (SW), graphene quantum dot (GQD), and two fullerenes (C60 and C70), covering different kinds of nanostructure, from 0-dimension to 3-dimension, were compared and investigated in different systems. We characterized the CBNs, examined their cellular biocompatibility in human kidney (293T) and lung (IMR90) cells, and investigated the cell cycle regulations and cell death pathways activation mediated by CBNs. The transcriptome profile of human cells exposed to CBNs was examined, and among all, ND exposed samples exhibited diverse regulatory patterns, and therefore the related complex network-and-pathway regulations of the CBN in human cells were further analyzed. In the zebrafish embryo model, all CBNs did not influence either the survival or hatching rates of the embryos, demonstrating the great biocompatibility of CBNs. This study evidences that carboxylated CBNs with various dimensional structures exhibited different molecular modulations toward human cells, and provides novel insights into the safety evaluations of CBNs for future applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: CBNs have been widely applied in various fields, such as being utilized alone or in hybrid materials in the industry [1], in the environmental applications [2], and in biomedical fields [3]. Different types of CBNs have been investigated for their great potential in medical applications, for example, developed as drug delivery carriers, applied for biosensing, or therapeutic imaging [4].

Applications such as drug carriers, imaging agents, diagnostic sensors or engineering materials that highlight the unique properties of CBNs are studied for over two decades [3,4]. In the field of drug delivery, 3D‑carbon diamonds which were conjugated with oligonucleotide and monoclonal antibodies could serve as a tumor-targeting platform for increased Paclitaxel drug capacity, leading to enhanced cellular cytotoxicity toward EGFR-overexpressed tumor cells [5]. Owing to its thermal tolerability and conductivity, nanocrystalline type of nanodiamond (ND) can be utilized as ideal medical advices (e.g. coatings for artificial joints) and alternative surgical implants [6,7]. The linear polyethylenimine (PEI) modified ND was also reported as promising strategy of in vitro gene delivery [8], while the nanocomplex of ND and doxorubicin was reported as efficient strategy against recurrent liver tumors [9]. NDs are suggested to exhibit unique features such as functionalization versatility, relative low cytotoxicity, high chemical stability, and good biocompatibility, etc. [10].

Graphene family are composed of single- or multiple-layer graphene or graphene oxide (GO), based on the number of layers. As a 2D‑carbon nanomaterial, layer structure provides feasibility of chemical modifications with functional groups. For instance, π-π transitions enable hydrophobic agents to conjugate on the graphene sheet and the modifications of a carboxyl group (-COOH) or hydroxyl group (-OH) allow stability in preparation of nanosuspensions [11]. For instance, polyethylene glycol (PEG)-ylated GO could serve as a vehicle for insoluble anticancer drugs [12]. A review article provided a considerable overview of graphene in development of cancer therapeutics and tissue engineering due to its remarkable large surface area with small size and electrical properties [13]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) also attract attention of scientists [14]. It was reported that apart from the potential contribution of serving as a drug delivery system [15], GQDs have exhibited potential in anti-diabetes and anti-bacterial ability, and could also potentially be used as a drug to benefit Parkinson's disease and Alzheimer's diseases [14].

Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are 1D‑carbon nanomaterials which are derived from different amount of graphene sheets, and categorized into single-walled (SW) or multiwalled (MW) carbon nanotubes. Similarly, with graphene, CNTs exhibit large surface area which allows a wide range of different functional groups or biomolecules to conjugate with [11]. Folic acid-functionalized CNTs with cisplatin or carboplatin showed greatly tumor remission in vitro [16,17]. To improve the solubility of hydrophobic chemo drugs, PEGylation or SWCNTs-conjugated Paclitaxel displayed enhanced solubility, compared to commercial chemo product named Taxol (Paclitaxel with Cremophor EL) [18].

Fullerenes have been investigated for biomedical applications since they were discovered in 1985 [19]. C60 is in a spherical shape which contains 60 carbon atoms. However, C60 has poor solubility [20]. Therefore, amphiphilic fullerenes which enhance hydrophilicity through PEGylation (e.g. C60-PEG), were employed to organic electronic field as an active cathode buffer layer with high efficiency and good stability [21]. Meanwhile, fullerenes could also incorporate with doxorubicin (DOX) to form the fullerene-DOX conjugates as an effective, pH-triggered nanocarrier for breast cancer [22]. Aside from vehicles for drug delivery toward cancer, fullerenes can function as an antioxidant agent to alleviate the DOX-induced nephrotoxicity [23], and modulate by hydrogels as a photodynamic therapeutic agent for antibacterial therapy [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Previous studies of impacts and potential toxicity of CBNs
ce:section.ce:para: Since there has been intensive application of CBNs in different fields, the utilization of these nanomaterials is increasing, and the related safety issue has been raised. There is an urgent need to understand how these nanomaterials might affect us human beings and also our environment [25]. Among different types of CBNs, CNTs were developed earlier and have had more related reports, including the comparison of SWCNTs and multi-wall CNTs, different lengths of them, and various functionalization methods, etc. [26]. There are also toxicity evaluation preclinical studies of other carbon nanomaterials in the recent years, and their resulted toxicity effects in various cells and different animal models include oxidative stress induction, metabolic pathway regulation, inflammation induction, neurotoxicity, and damage of the lung tissue, etc. [25,27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gaps in knowledge and the rationale of this study
ce:section.ce:para: Scientific data and useful information have been provided in the above studies regarding the toxicity examination of these nanomaterials, unfortunately, it is difficult and can be a big problem to compare these results, between different cell lines and different animal models. Moreover, no one has investigated the functional, molecular, and genetic regulations of different CBNs within different systems at the same time.

In this current study, six types of carbon nanomaterials were applied, including nanodiamond (ND), graphene (Gra), single-wall carbon nanotube (SW), graphene quantum dot (GQD), and two fullerenes (C60 and C70), cover different kinds of nanostructure, from 3 dimension to 0 dimension. Cellular regulatory mechanisms as well as the complex network-and-pathway regulations of CBNs were revealed for the first time.

CBNs include highly purified powder of carboxyl SW, Gra, and dispersion liquid of GQD were obtained from XF NANO (China). Carboxylated C60 powder and C70 powder were purchased from UniRegion Bio-Tech (Taiwan), while liquid carboxylated monodispersed ND dispersion was commercially obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (USA).
The diameter and size distribution of different CBNs were evaluated through Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM, HT7700, Hitachi, Japan).

Human kidney cells 293 T and human fibroblast IMR90 cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM, Gibco, USA) consisting of 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS, Gibco, USA) in addition with 1 % penicillin/streptomycin antibiotics (Hyclone, USA) and incubated in 5 % CO2 atmosphere under 37 °C [28]. Trypsin-EDTA was also from Gibco.

Cells were seeded in a 96-well plate at a density of 5 × 103 cells in 100 μL culture medium per well. After incubation for 24 h, cells were treated with various concentrations of carboxylated CBNs and incubated for another 24 h. After the indicated period of time, MTT (3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5 diphenyltetrazolium bromide) labeling reagent was then added to each well for incubation. After 4 h of incubation, the medium was carefully removed and then DMSO (Dimethyl sulfoxide) as the solubilization solution was added into each well. After complete solubilization of the purple formazan crystals, Epoch Microplate Spectrophotometer was utilized to measure the absorbance at the wavelength of 570 nm [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell cycle analysis by flow cytometer
ce:section.ce:para: Cells (2.5 × 105/well) were co-incubated with 20 μg/mL of CBNs in 6-well plate for 24 h. The collected cells were washed with cold PBS and following fixed with 70 % (v/v) pre-chilled ethanol under viciously stirring, then stored at −20 °C. The next day, cells were stained with propidium iodide (PI, Biotium, USA) and analyzed by flow cytometer (FACS Caliber, BD, USA) [30].

Cells were harvested with 20 μg/mL of CBNs for 24 h, and then cells were homogenized using an ice-cold lysis buffer containing protease inhibitors. Protein lysates were quantified by Bradford protein assay (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), and 30 μg of protein from each sample was denatured by boiling with 4× protein sample buffer at 95 °C for 5 min. Denatured proteins were electrophoresed through SDS-PAGE gel and then transferred to PVDF membranes, blocking, and then probed for antibodies. After the antibody incubation and washing steps, the membranes were incubated for a few seconds in the dark with the substrate working solution which is mixed by equal parts of the peroxide solution and the Luminol Enhancer Solution. Protein signals were detected by GeneSys and the exposure time was increased to achieve optimal results [31]. The antibodies against PARP, GAPDH, Cyclin-B1, CDK2, and p21 were purchased from GeneTex (CA, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: RNA extraction, RNA quality evaluation, and RNA sequencing (RNA-seq) analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Cells after the treatments were harvested, RNA extraction was carried out with Total RNA Mini Kit (Viogene, Taiwan) according to manufacturer's instructions. RNA concentration was examined by nanodrop 2000 spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific, USA), and then samples went through RNA separation with agarose gel electrophoresis to exclude the severely degraded ones. RNA samples were sent for further quality check and RNA-seq analysis (Illumina platform, Phalanx Biotech Group, Taiwan). Analysis Reference: Emsembl Homo_sapiens.GRCh38.104. Clustering Analysis were presented by Heatmap, Volcano Plot; Functional Enrichment Analysis were carried out from KEGG (Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes) and GO (Gene Ontology) databases, and STRING protein-protein interaction software (Version 12.0) (https://string-db.org/).

To examine the in vivo toxicological regulation in an animal model, zebrafish (Danio rerio) embryo and larvae were introduced, to analyze the possible effects of CBNs on zebrafish development by assessing the embryogenesis period [32]. As to the regulation of animal welfare, zebrafish embryos before the onset of independent feeding (younger than 120 h post fertilization (hpf)) are considered as nonprotected life stages by the current European Directive 2010/63/EU on the protection of animals in scientific research application [33,34] (EU Directive 2010/63/EU of the European parliament and of the council of 22 September 2010 on the protection of animals used for scientific purposes Off. J. Eur. Union, L 2010, L 276, 33–79).

All experimental data were presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD) and analyzed by GraphPad Prism 8 software. Group comparison was performed by Student's t-test or one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA), and the difference was considered statistically significant when p values <0.05 [35,36].

We applied CBNs that were functionalized with carboxyl group as described in materials and methods in this study. In order to characterize their structure, size, and distribution, we utilized the transmission electron microscope (TEM) for the analysis (Fig. 1). The various dark grey shades illustrated the differences in penetration of high-velocity electron beam, and further confirmed the three-dimensional structure of ND. The images shown here presented typical morphological identification of these nanomaterials, including the crystalline structure of the nanodiamond (ND), the length-sorted single-walled carbon nanotube bundle (SW), plate-like shapes of graphene sheet (Gra), the dot-shape of graphene quantum dots (GQD), and fullerenes with hollow sphere (C60 and C70) (Fig. 1). To further characterize the properties of CBNs, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and zeta potential measurements were performed (Supplementary Fig. 1). Supplementary Fig. 1A illustrates FTIR spectra of all CBNs, consistently exhibited a characteristic peak at approximately 1726 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of the carbonyl (C=O) bond. This observation confirms the presence of carboxyl (-COOH) functional groups on all the purchased carbon materials, validating their chemical functionalization. Moreover, in the Supplementary Fig. 1B, zeta potential measurements were conducted to evaluate the surface charge characteristics of various CBNs. It shows that all tested CBNs, including SWCNT(SW)-COOH, Graphene(Gra)-COOH, C60-COOH, C70-COOH, ND-COOH, and GQDs-COOH, exhibited negative zeta potentials, primarily due to the presence of negatively charged carboxyl groups on their surfaces. These complementary analyses, along with electron microscopy, support the characterization of the carboxylated CBNs employed in this study.

We applied CBNs that were functionalized with carboxyl group as described in materials and methods in this study. In order to characterize their structure, size, and distribution, we utilized the transmission electron microscope (TEM) for the analysis (Fig. 1). The various dark grey shades illustrated the differences in penetration of high-velocity electron beam, and further confirmed the three-dimensional structure of ND. The images shown here presented typical morphological identification of these nanomaterials, including the crystalline structure of the nanodiamond (ND), the length-sorted single-walled carbon nanotube bundle (SW), plate-like shapes of graphene sheet (Gra), the dot-shape of graphene quantum dots (GQD), and fullerenes with hollow sphere (C60 and C70) (Fig. 1). To further characterize the properties of CBNs, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and zeta potential measurements were performed (Supplementary Fig. 1). Supplementary Fig. 1A illustrates FTIR spectra of all CBNs, consistently exhibited a characteristic peak at approximately 1726 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of the carbonyl (C=O) bond. This observation confirms the presence of carboxyl (-COOH) functional groups on all the purchased carbon materials, validating their chemical functionalization. Moreover, in the Supplementary Fig. 1B, zeta potential measurements were conducted to evaluate the surface charge characteristics of various CBNs. It shows that all tested CBNs, including SWCNT(SW)-COOH, Graphene(Gra)-COOH, C60-COOH, C70-COOH, ND-COOH, and GQDs-COOH, exhibited negative zeta potentials, primarily due to the presence of negatively charged carboxyl groups on their surfaces. These complementary analyses, along with electron microscopy, support the characterization of the carboxylated CBNs employed in this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility evaluation of CBNs in human cells
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate how these CBNs could affect human cells, firstly, we performed MTT cell viability assay. We introduced non-cancerous human kidney 293 T and lung fibroblast IMR90 cells in the assay, cells were co-incubated with CBNs at concentrations of 1, 10 and 20 μg/mL for 24 h, and then cell viability was analyzed by MTT assay (Fig. 2 ). The results showed that compared to untreated control groups, cell viability in the SW treated group seemed to be lower in both 293 T and IMR90 cells (Fig. 2AC). Other CBNs generally caused little effect towards human cells at low concentrations, and the results were slightly different in 293T and IMR90 cells (Fig. 2AC), with 293T cells being more sensitive toward CBNs treatments, we then applied 293T cells in the later experiments. Cell viability remained to be around or higher than 75 % of the control for all of the CBNs treated cells, suggested that these CBNs at these concentrations exhibit tolerable cell compatibility in human cell lines. Cell morphology of control and treated cells were monitored under the microscopy and shown here (Fig. 2B and D).

Our results indicated that SW treatment could cause cell death in pulmonary cells, kidney cells, or other cells, which is compatible with previously described [37] as well as what was discovered in our laboratory [38]. Apart from SW, ND or fullerenes co-incubation could also lead to a lower cell viability (Fig. 2A and C). Since SW is a relatively older nanomaterial that has been investigated for years as mentioned, we then performed a longer time point (72 h) MTT assay in 293 T cells with incubation of other CBNs (Supplementary Fig. 2). Our data indicate that incubation for 72 h with a higher concentration (10 or 20 μg/mL) of these CBNs in cells resulted in high cell viability compared to the control group (Supplementary Fig. 2A). The treatments of C60, C70, or GQD for 72 h did not influence the viability of cells. For cells under the 72 h incubation of ND or GO, cell viability remained 75 % or higher (Supplementary Fig. 2A). The above results suggest a great biocompatibility of most CBNs even at a longer treatment time point. We did not observe significant changes in cell morphology in any group (Supplementary Fig. 2B).

Our results indicated that SW treatment could cause cell death in pulmonary cells, kidney cells, or other cells, which is compatible with previously described [37] as well as what was discovered in our laboratory [38]. Apart from SW, ND or fullerenes co-incubation could also lead to a lower cell viability (Fig. 2A and C). Since SW is a relatively older nanomaterial that has been investigated for years as mentioned, we then performed a longer time point (72 h) MTT assay in 293 T cells with incubation of other CBNs (Supplementary Fig. 2). Our data indicate that incubation for 72 h with a higher concentration (10 or 20 μg/mL) of these CBNs in cells resulted in high cell viability compared to the control group (Supplementary Fig. 2A). The treatments of C60, C70, or GQD for 72 h did not influence the viability of cells. For cells under the 72 h incubation of ND or GO, cell viability remained 75 % or higher (Supplementary Fig. 2A). The above results suggest a great biocompatibility of most CBNs even at a longer treatment time point. We did not observe significant changes in cell morphology in any group (Supplementary Fig. 2B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular and molecular regulations of CBNs in cells
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the possible regulation and influence of these CBNs toward cell cycle profile, we applied flow cytometry to examine 293 T cells under treatment of CBNs. Cells were co-incubated with 20 μg/mL CBNs for 24 h, and then harvested for flow cytometry analysis. The histogram of cell-cycle distribution and statistical analysis revealed differ regulations in each CBN treatment in comparison with untreated control group (Fig. 3A). The quantitative bar chart showed the percentage of cell populations in each cell cycle stage of different groups (Fig. 3B). The data suggests that all these CBNs could regulate cell cycle profiles at different levels, for example, the treatments of ND, SW, and C60 seemed to result in slight G1 arrest, and the treatment of graphene lead to slight G2/M phase arrest in cells (Fig. 3).

Next, to further research the intracellular mechanism underlying the cell suppressive effect mediated by CBNs co-incubation, western blot assay was applied to examine protein expression of 293 T cells that have been treated with 20 μg/mL of CBNs for 24 h. As we have observed cell viability reduction and cell cycle regulation under the CBNs treatment (Fig. 2 and Fig. 3), expression levels of selected death pathway markers and cell cycle-related markers were applied for western blot analysis. The results showed that treatment of certain CBN(s), such as SW, induced mild PARP cleavage, a marker of apoptosis pathway in cells, while as treatments of CBN(s), such as ND, Gra, GQD, and C60, led to induction of ATG7, a marker of autophagy pathway in cells (Fig. 4A). Treatments with most of the CBNs also led to induction of expression levels of cell cycle regulatory factors, CDK2 and cyclin B1 (Fig. 4B).

The induction of cell death pathway markers is in consistency with our results of MTT assay (Fig. 2), while as activation of CDK2 and cyclin B1 cell cycle factors suggested a possible cell cycle arrest mediated by CBNs treatment, which is consistent with data from flow cytometry assay (Fig. 3) [39–41]. Even though co-incubation of cells with most CBNs could lead to the above effects, and treatments of C70 also led to cell viability reduction, however, compared to other CBNs, C70 did not seem to significantly regulate examined markers in either death pathways or cell cycle regulatory factors, indicating that it might exhibit a better biocompatibility in human cells, or it might regulate cells through different targets or pathways.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RNA-sequencing analysis of human cells treated with CBNs
ce:section.ce:para: According to data from Fig. 3 and Fig. 4, the molecular regulatory effects of CBNs can be complicated and vary in different nanomaterials. To investigate the overall genetic regulation of these carbon nanomaterials toward human cells, 293 T cells were treated with CBNs, and then RNAs were extracted from cells in each group for RNA-sequencing analysis as described in materials and methods. In order to evaluate and visualize the expression difference among various sample conditions, for example, the control group and the treatment groups, principal component analysis (PCA) was performed, and the PCA plot analysis revealed that ND group is far away from all the other groups (Fig. 5 ), suggesting that the gene expression profile of ND group is significantly different from all other groups.

We also visualized our RNA-seq data using a volcano plot, a type of scatterplot that highlights the statistical significance (P value on the x-axis) against the magnitude of change (fold change on the y-axis), commonly employed to present the outcomes of omics experiments. It allows the visual identification of genes that exhibit both large fold changes and high statistical significance, which are often the most biologically relevant. In this plot, highly upregulated genes appear on the right, highly downregulated genes on the left, and the most statistically significant genes are positioned at the top (Fig. 7 , blue dots). The significant genes of each treatment compared to the control group are displayed under the same threshold settings, and among all CBNs-treated samples, ND-treated sample led to a significant amount of genetic changes (Fig. 7), suggesting that wide and strong genetic regulations in human cells can be modulated by ND treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Genetic regulations of human cells mediated by ND treatment
ce:section.ce:para: The 58 significant protein-encoded genes within above mentioned cell cycle pathway identified include factors that can regulate cell cycle profile and also influence various cellular mechanisms, such as ecotropic viral integration site 5 (EVI5) [42], a GTPase activator, and cyclin D binding myb like transcription factor 1 (DMTF1) [43,44], a transcription factor, that can modulate cell cycle progression or growth arrest; molecules that can regulate not only cell cycle but also apoptosis pathway, for example, a proapoptotic protein, caspase 8 associated protein 2 (CASP8AP2) [45,46], and the mitochondrial ribosomal protein L41 (MRPL41), a bcl-2 binding protein [47,48]; retinoblastoma-like 1 (RBL1) and retinoblastoma-like 2 (RBL2) that would encode proteins named p107 and p130, respectively, and share structural and functional features with pRB, the famous tumor suppressor, in regulating cell cycle [49]. The results display the complexity of the network-and-pathway regulations. Moreover, this data is compatible with Fig. 3 and Fig. 4 and suggested a complex regulation of cell cycle and cell death pathway mediated by ND treatment. Above examples are differ genes in ND treated cells, yet, other CBNs could also influence various genetic patterns compared to control cells at a moderate level.

This is the first time we demonstrate that so many cell cycle molecules can be influenced by CBNs under the indicated conditions. RNA-sequencing analysis provided us a big picture of the regulation of CBNs in cells, and it was surprising to find out that ND exhibited a significantly different genetic pattern compared to control group. The relationship between the structural dimension of CBNs and their effects on cells needs to be further verified and investigated by more experiments. For instance, with several human cell lines and also in primary cells applied, and a wide variety of nanomaterials and concentrations examined, to help us in illustrating the effects of CBNs in living cells; animal models would also be helpful for identifying potential harmful effects in tissues and organs, and a relative safe concentration that can be applied in clinical studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility examination of CBNs in the zebrafish model
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the in vivo biocompatibility of these CBNs, we applied the zebrafish embryos for the zebrafish embryotoxicity test, which had been recommended as a vertebrate model for chemical or nanotoxicological examination, to investigate the possibility of malformations in parallel with delayed growth [50–52].

Here, we designed a survival analysis through zebrafish embryos during the period of embryogenesis under CBNs treatment with concentrations of 10 and 20 μg/mL (n = 30 zebrafish embryos per group). The results revealed that all CBN-treated (20 μg/mL) groups had similar survival rates in comparison with control group (Fig. 9A), representing high tolerability and biocompatibility of CBNs in zebrafish embryos. Next, to monitor the effect of CBNs on the embryonic stages within fertilization of zebrafish embryos, we calculated the ratio of hatched and unhatched zebrafish embryos under exposure of CBNs. The results proved no significant difference in cumulative hatching rate among all embryos exposed to 20 μg/mL of CBNs compared to the control group (Fig. 9B). We further examined the body length of zebrafish larvae, and no observable differences in body length were observed under 10 or 20 μg/mL concentrations of CBNs (Fig. 9C), suggesting that none of the samples resulted in malformations or body length changes. Moreover, as it was previously suggested that CBNs could accumulate at the surface of embryos, we wondered whether bioaccumulation inside the zebrafish larvae might be possible. Under the microscope observation, Gra-treated larvae were found vulnerable to accumulate in gastrointestinal (GI) tract, whereas other CBNs seemed no obvious bioaccumulation (Fig. 9D). Nevertheless, in the future, it is warranted to investigate these CBNs in regards with biodistribution and bioaccumulation in more comprehensive and methodical ways since several samples such as GQDs are too colorless to be observed by naked eyes.

In this study, we characterized six CBNs ranging from 0D to 3D. Structure of CBNs was characterized by TEM, FTIR, and zeta potential measurements. The cellular, molecular, and genetic regulations of these CBNs were investigated, and we compared and demonstrated the first time how different CBNs can exhibit differ influences toward human cells and in the zebrafish model. Our data suggests that CBNs could regulate cell cycle arrest and induce apoptosis effects in cells. Compatibly, RNA-sequencing analysis revealed that among these CBNs, incubation of ND regulated complex network-and-pathway interactions of molecules modulating cell cycle and apoptosis mechanisms in cells. Even though results from zebrafish experiments suggested that CBNs did not affect either the survival or hatching rates of the zebrafish embryos, it is possible that CBNs could accumulate in the larvae, so further investigation would be critical. In the future, a deeper genetic and proteomics analysis would provide us a comprehensive view of how these CBNs can impact on us, facilitate the diverse yet safe application of CBNs as well as for the better protection of the environment.

The following are the supplementary data related to this article.
Supplementary Fig. 1
Characterization of carboxylated carbon-based nanomaterials (CBNs). (A) FTIR spectra of CBNs. (B) Zeta potential spectra of CBNs.
Supplementary Fig. 1
Supplementary Fig. 2
Cell viability assay with nanomaterials incubation in 293T human cells. Cells were treated with indicated nanomaterials for 72 h, and then subjected for MTT assay for cell viability analysis (A). Cell morphology was also examined under the microscope (B). The scale bar stands for 100 μm. p value < 0.05 was considered statistically significant.
Supplementary Fig. 2
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2025.111946.

Min Li: Writing – original draft, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Validation. Chien Chang: Investigation, Software. Tseng-Yu Yeh: Software, Investigation. Tung-Yu Lee: Software, Investigation. Kuen-Chan Lee: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Supervision, Methodology. Er-Chieh Cho: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: EC Cho and KC Lee reports financial support was provided by National Science and Technology Council. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Science and Technology Council (NSTC 113-2221-E-152-001, NSTC 112-2628-B-038-009-MY3), Taiwan. This work was also supported by the “TMU Research Center of Cancer Translational Medicine” from The Featured Areas Research Center Program within the framework of the Higher Education Sprout Project by the Ministry of Education (MOE) in Taiwan. We thank the Core Facility Centers at Taipei Medical University and National Taipei University of Education for the technical supports. We would also like to thank the Core Laboratory of Zebrafish, Office of R&D at Taipei Medical University for providing the wild type fish.
